entries.title: Add column (INSTANT)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c INT [DEFAULT ...] , ALGORITHM=INSTANT
entries.lockMode: NONE (no metadata-lock upgrade beyond the brief exclusive MDL every DDL takes)
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c INT DEFAULT 0, ALGORITHM=INSTANT, LOCK=NONE;
safeWhen: Table is not ROW_FORMAT=COMPRESSED and has no FULLTEXT index
Column can be added at any position (8.0.29+ allows non-trailing; pre-8.0.29 INSTANT only appends at end)
Fewer than 64 instant-column row versions have accumulated since the last full rebuild
unsafeWhen: Table already has 64 accumulated instant row versions (ALTER errors; needs a rebuild e.g. OPTIMIZE/FORCE to reset)
ROW_FORMAT=COMPRESSED or a FULLTEXT/temp/system table (INSTANT unsupported -> use INPLACE, which rebuilds)
Adding an AUTO_INCREMENT column (never INSTANT; see add-auto-increment-column)
edgeCases: INSTANT is the default algorithm for ADD COLUMN since 8.0.12; server picks it automatically unless you force otherwise
The change is metadata-only: existing rows are NOT rewritten, the default is materialized lazily on read
You cannot mix an INSTANT operation with a non-INSTANT one in the same ALTER statement
versionNotes: 8.0.12: INSTANT introduced (append-only). 8.0.29: add column at any position + drop column made INSTANT, but each such op consumes one of the 64 row versions.

PostgreSQL contrast: PG 11+ also adds a column with a (even non-constant in 11, constant-only fast path) default as a catalog-only change; but PG DDL is transactional and rollback-able mid-statement, MySQL DDL is NOT (no DDL transaction, partial failure can leave #sql temp artifacts).

entries.title: Add column when INSTANT is unavailable (INPLACE, rebuilds)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c ..., ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c INT, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;  -- table is rebuilt online; dwell scales with size
safeWhen: INSTANT is blocked (COMPRESSED row format, FULLTEXT present, or 64-version cap hit) but online rebuild is acceptable
There is enough free disk for a full second copy of the table + sort files
unsafeWhen: Disk headroom is under ~1.2x table size (rebuild needs a full copy)
Long-running transactions hold the table: the final MDL upgrade at commit can stall behind them and then stall everything else
edgeCases: Even INPLACE add-column rebuilds the clustered index, so cost scales with table size despite LOCK=NONE
innodb_online_alter_log_max_size governs the online change buffer; overflow aborts the ALTER
versionNotes: Prefer INSTANT; only falls back to this INPLACE rebuild path when INSTANT preconditions are not met.
For very large tables where even LOCK=NONE dwell (and the commit-time MDL) is risky, fall back to gh-ost/pt-osc for controlled, throttled, resumable copy.

entries.title: Drop column
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN c, ALGORITHM=INSTANT
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN c, ALGORITHM=INSTANT, LOCK=NONE;  -- 8.0.29+; else INPLACE rebuild
safeWhen: MySQL 8.0.29+ and INSTANT preconditions hold (no COMPRESSED/FULLTEXT, under 64 row-version cap): metadata-only
Pre-8.0.29 or INSTANT blocked: INPLACE with LOCK=NONE is still online but REWRITES the table
unsafeWhen: Dropping a column that is part of an index/FK in a way that forces additional index rebuilds
64-version cap reached (INSTANT errors -> falls to rebuild)
edgeCases: INSTANT drop keeps the physical column data on disk (hidden) until the next full rebuild; consider OPTIMIZE TABLE periodically to reclaim space and reset row-version count
versionNotes: 8.0.29 made DROP COLUMN eligible for ALGORITHM=INSTANT; before that it is INPLACE and rebuilds.

PostgreSQL contrast: PG DROP COLUMN is ALWAYS catalog-only (attisdropped) and never rewrites, at any version, but takes a brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock. MySQL only reaches metadata-only on 8.0.29+ and still counts against the 64-version cap.

entries.title: Rename column
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t CHANGE old new SAME_TYPE, ALGORITHM=INSTANT
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t CHANGE old_name new_name INT NOT NULL, ALGORITHM=INSTANT, LOCK=NONE;  -- data type MUST stay identical
safeWhen: The data type in the CHANGE clause is byte-for-byte the same as the current one (only the name changes)
MySQL 8.0.28+ for INSTANT; earlier versions do it INPLACE (still metadata-only, LOCK=NONE)
unsafeWhen: The renamed column is referenced by a foreign key, generated column, or view expression (rename may be blocked or require ALGORITHM=COPY)
You change the type at the same time -> that is a data-type change (COPY), not a rename
edgeCases: Renaming a column used in a functional index or CHECK constraint can force COPY
versionNotes: 8.0.28+: rename column eligible for INSTANT. Pre-8.0.28: INPLACE, metadata-only, LOCK=NONE.
PostgreSQL contrast: PG ALTER TABLE ... RENAME COLUMN is always a pure catalog change (brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE), independent of type.

entries.title: Reorder column (AFTER / FIRST)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c INT AFTER x, ALGORITHM=INPLACE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c INT AFTER x, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;  -- rebuilds table
safeWhen: Online rebuild dwell is acceptable and disk headroom exists
unsafeWhen: Very large table under heavy write load -> use gh-ost/pt-osc for throttled copy
edgeCases: Physical reordering forces a clustered-index rebuild; cost scales with size even though LOCK=NONE
versionNotes: Never INSTANT: physical row layout changes.
PostgreSQL contrast: PG has no column reorder at all (column order is fixed by catalog attnum); the operation simply does not exist, so a multi-DB abstraction must gate it as MySQL-only.

entries.title: Set / drop column DEFAULT
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c SET DEFAULT 5, ALGORITHM=INSTANT
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c SET DEFAULT 5, ALGORITHM=INSTANT, LOCK=NONE;
safeWhen: Always safe: touches only the data dictionary, existing rows unaffected
edgeCases: Changing the default does NOT backfill existing rows; only future inserts see the new default
versionNotes: Metadata-only at all supported 8.0 versions.
PostgreSQL contrast: identical semantics (catalog-only, brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE); one of the few operations that behave the same in both.

entries.title: Change column data type (INT->BIGINT, DECIMAL precision, etc.)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c BIGINT, ALGORITHM=COPY
entries.lockMode: SHARED (LOCK=NONE not permitted; reads allowed, writes blocked)
entries.safeRewrite:
-- Native DDL blocks writes for the whole copy. For a large hot table, run through an online tool:
gh-ost --alter="MODIFY c BIGINT" ... ;  -- or pt-online-schema-change --alter "MODIFY c BIGINT"
safeWhen: Table is small enough that a write-blocking COPY finishes inside your maintenance window
Or you route it through gh-ost / pt-osc for zero-write-downtime
unsafeWhen: Large, write-heavy table with native ALTER: LOCK is at least SHARED for the entire rebuild, blocking all writes for the copy duration
Narrowing a type where existing data would be truncated/out of range (fails or silently coerces depending on SQL mode)
edgeCases: Almost all real type changes are ALGORITHM=COPY and cannot be LOCK=NONE -> this is the #1 reason teams reach for gh-ost/pt-osc
Changing a column's character set or collation is likewise a rebuild
versionNotes: General type change = COPY, no INPLACE, no LOCK=NONE. (Special cases like VARCHAR extension and ENUM append are separate entries and ARE online.)

PostgreSQL contrast: PG ALTER COLUMN TYPE also rewrites but takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (blocks READS too) -- strictly worse than MySQL's SHARED. PG mitigations differ (e.g. add new col + backfill + swap, or binary-coercible no-rewrite cases like varchar(n)->text).

entries.title: Extend VARCHAR length (in place)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c VARCHAR(255), ALGORITHM=INPLACE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c VARCHAR(255), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;  -- only when the length-prefix byte count stays the same
safeWhen: Enlarging within the same length-prefix size class: 0-255 bytes uses a 1-byte length prefix, 256+ uses 2 bytes. Growing within a class (e.g. 20 -> 100 for latin1) is metadata-only INPLACE.
unsafeWhen: Crossing the 255/256-byte boundary (e.g. VARCHAR(200)->VARCHAR(300) in a single-byte charset, or the utf8mb4 equivalent at ~63/64 chars) -> length prefix grows from 1 to 2 bytes -> requires ALGORITHM=COPY, write-blocking
SHRINKING a VARCHAR is always COPY (never in place)
edgeCases: For utf8mb4 (4 bytes/char) the boundary is ~63 vs 64 characters, not 255 -- easy to trip accidentally
If you cross the boundary and force ALGORITHM=INPLACE, the statement errors rather than silently copying
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG increasing varchar(n) length (or dropping the limit) is a pure catalog change with no rewrite and no boundary rule; MySQL's byte-prefix boundary is a MySQL-specific hazard the abstraction must special-case.

entries.title: Make column NULLable (drop NOT NULL)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c INT NULL, ALGORITHM=INPLACE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c INT NULL, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;  -- rebuilds table
safeWhen: Online rebuild dwell acceptable; disk headroom exists
unsafeWhen: Large hot table -> gh-ost/pt-osc
edgeCases: Rebuilds even though it seems trivial, because the InnoDB row format encodes nullability
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG DROP NOT NULL is catalog-only (brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE), no rewrite -- MySQL rebuilds. Divergent cost class for the same logical change.

entries.title: Add NOT NULL to a column
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c INT NOT NULL, ALGORITHM=INPLACE
entries.safeRewrite:
SET SESSION sql_mode='STRICT_ALL_TABLES';
ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c INT NOT NULL, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;  -- rebuilds; requires strict SQL mode
safeWhen: STRICT_TRANS_TABLES or STRICT_ALL_TABLES is enabled AND no existing row holds NULL in the column
Online rebuild dwell acceptable
unsafeWhen: A row already contains NULL (ALTER fails under strict mode; without strict mode NULLs get coerced to the type default -- silent data change)
Non-strict SQL mode -> INPLACE not permitted, coercion risk
edgeCases: Rebuilds the table; combine with a validating pre-check: SELECT 1 FROM t WHERE c IS NULL LIMIT 1;
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG SET NOT NULL scans the table (SHARE ... lock) but does not rewrite; PG 12+ can skip the scan if a matching CHECK (c IS NOT NULL) constraint was pre-validated with NOT VALID + VALIDATE. MySQL always rebuilds and has no NOT VALID equivalent.

entries.title: Append values to ENUM / SET
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c ENUM('a','b','c'), ALGORITHM=INSTANT
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t MODIFY c ENUM('a','b','c','d'), ALGORITHM=INSTANT, LOCK=NONE;  -- append only, at the END, without shrinking storage size
safeWhen: Adding members to the END of the ENUM/SET list without exceeding the storage-size boundary (ENUM: 1 byte up to 255 members, 2 bytes beyond)
unsafeWhen: Reordering, removing, or renaming members, or inserting in the middle -> changes stored numeric codes -> ALGORITHM=COPY and a data rewrite
Crossing the 255-member (1->2 byte) boundary -> COPY
edgeCases: ENUM values are stored as ordinals; any change that shifts ordinals is a full rewrite
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG enums are a first-class type; ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE is catalog-only but cannot run inside a transaction block and (pre-12) the new value could not be used in the same tx. Very different model -- MySQL ENUM is per-column, PG is a shared type.

entries.title: Add secondary (B-tree) index
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD INDEX ix (c), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t ADD INDEX ix_t_c (c), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;
safeWhen: Standard secondary index add: INPLACE, LOCK=NONE, does NOT rebuild the clustered table (builds the index via sort + online log)
innodb_online_alter_log_max_size is large enough to absorb DML during the build
unsafeWhen: Adding a UNIQUE index when duplicate data exists (fails at build/apply time)
The online change log overflows during a long build on a write-heavy table (ALTER aborts) -> raise innodb_online_alter_log_max_size or use gh-ost/pt-osc
Adding an index as part of an ALTER that also does a COPY operation -> whole statement becomes COPY
edgeCases: Brief exclusive MDL at start and end; the middle (the scan/sort) is concurrent
This is the canonical 'online' operation and the closest MySQL analog to PG CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG needs CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (CIC) to avoid a write lock; CIC does TWO table scans, cannot run in a transaction, and can leave an INVALID index on failure that must be dropped/rebuilt. MySQL's online index build is a single statement with automatic cleanup on failure -- generally easier to operate.

entries.title: Drop secondary index
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP INDEX ix, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t DROP INDEX ix_t_c, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;
safeWhen: Always online and metadata-only (just unlinks the index from the data dictionary)
unsafeWhen: Dropping an index that backs a foreign key -> blocked until you drop the FK (or the FK is on another index)
edgeCases: Space is freed asynchronously
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG DROP INDEX takes a brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the table; use DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY to avoid blocking. MySQL drop-index is already non-blocking.

entries.title: Rename index
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t RENAME INDEX a TO b, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t RENAME INDEX old_ix TO new_ix, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;
safeWhen: Always metadata-only
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG ALTER INDEX ... RENAME TO is catalog-only as well; behavior matches.

entries.title: Add FULLTEXT index
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD FULLTEXT (c), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=SHARED
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t ADD FULLTEXT INDEX ft_c (c), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=SHARED;
safeWhen: Reads may continue; you accept writes being blocked for the build
unsafeWhen: The FIRST fulltext index on a table with no user-defined FTS_DOC_ID column forces a full TABLE REBUILD to add the hidden FTS_DOC_ID (much heavier + still LOCK=SHARED). Subsequent fulltext indexes are cheaper.
Write availability required during build -> route through gh-ost/pt-osc
edgeCases: LOCK=NONE is rejected for FULLTEXT; minimum is SHARED
versionNotes: Define a column named FTS_DOC_ID BIGINT UNSIGNED NOT NULL up front to avoid the first-fulltext rebuild.
PostgreSQL contrast: PG full-text uses GIN/GiST on tsvector and CAN be built with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (writes allowed). MySQL blocks writes for fulltext -- a real divergence in availability.

entries.title: Add SPATIAL index
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD SPATIAL INDEX (g), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=SHARED
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t ADD SPATIAL INDEX sp_g (g), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=SHARED;
safeWhen: Reads continue; writes blocked during build is acceptable
unsafeWhen: Write availability required -> gh-ost/pt-osc
edgeCases: Column must be NOT NULL to be spatially indexed
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG spatial (GiST/PostGIS) indexes support CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY without blocking writes.

entries.title: Add PRIMARY KEY
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD PRIMARY KEY (c), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t ADD PRIMARY KEY (c), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;  -- rebuilds entire table (reclusters every row)
safeWhen: The PK columns are already NOT NULL and hold no duplicates
Strict SQL mode enabled; disk headroom for a full copy exists; rebuild dwell acceptable
unsafeWhen: A PK column contains NULLs: with ALGORITHM=COPY, NULLs are silently converted to type defaults (0/''/0000-00-00) -- data corruption risk. Pre-validate.
Very large table: reclustering rewrites 100% of rows -> strongly prefer gh-ost/pt-osc for throttling + resumability
edgeCases: Adding a PK is the single most expensive 'online' op because the clustered index IS the table; every secondary index is also rebuilt
Best practice: define the PK in CREATE TABLE, never add later
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG ADD PRIMARY KEY takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and scans to build a unique index (blocking reads+writes) unless you first CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY then ADD PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX. MySQL keeps LOCK=NONE but pays a full physical recluster; different trade-off.

entries.title: Drop PRIMARY KEY (without adding a new one)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP PRIMARY KEY, ALGORITHM=COPY
entries.safeRewrite:
-- Avoid dropping a PK alone. If you must swap it, drop+add in one statement (INPLACE):
ALTER TABLE t DROP PRIMARY KEY, ADD PRIMARY KEY (new_col), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;
safeWhen: Rarely safe on a live table; only in a maintenance window on a small table
unsafeWhen: Dropping the PK with nothing to recluster on forces ALGORITHM=COPY and blocks writes for the whole rebuild
InnoDB will fall back to a hidden 6-byte GEN_CLUST_INDEX -- almost never what you want
edgeCases: Combine drop+add into ONE ALTER to stay INPLACE/LOCK=NONE (separate entry: drop-add-primary-key)
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG DROP CONSTRAINT for a PK is catalog-only (brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE) and does NOT rewrite -- MySQL's COPY rewrite is far heavier.

entries.title: Swap PRIMARY KEY (drop + add in one statement)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP PRIMARY KEY, ADD PRIMARY KEY (c2), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t DROP PRIMARY KEY, ADD PRIMARY KEY (c2), ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;  -- rebuilds table
safeWhen: New PK columns are NOT NULL and unique; combining into one statement keeps it INPLACE
Rebuild dwell + disk headroom acceptable
unsafeWhen: Splitting into two statements (drop then add) forces the drop to be COPY/write-blocking
Huge table -> gh-ost/pt-osc (note: gh-ost CANNOT change the PK; pt-osc can if a suitable unique key exists)
edgeCases: gh-ost explicitly refuses primary-key changes; pt-osc can do it but needs an existing PK/unique index for its DELETE trigger unless the new PK is built from existing columns

entries.title: Add FOREIGN KEY
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT fk FOREIGN KEY (c) REFERENCES p(id), ALGORITHM=INPLACE
safeWhen: foreign_key_checks=0: INPLACE, LOCK=NONE, no full validation scan (metadata + index requirement) -- BUT you are trusting existing data is referentially valid
A usable index already exists on the referencing column (else one is created)
unsafeWhen: foreign_key_checks=1 (default): adding the FK requires ALGORITHM=COPY -> write-blocking rebuild
Existing rows violate the constraint while checks are off -> you create an invalid/unenforceable relationship silently
edgeCases: Online DDL on a table involved in FK CASCADE/SET NULL can stall waiting on transactions touching the related table
gh-ost does NOT support FKs at all; pt-osc handles them via --alter-foreign-keys-method (rebuild_constraints | drop_swap | none | auto)
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG can ADD FOREIGN KEY ... NOT VALID (fast, metadata, takes SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE briefly) then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (scans referencing rows, only ROW SHARE lock). MySQL has no NOT VALID -- it either validates fully (COPY) or skips validation entirely (checks off). This NOT VALID / VALIDATE two-step is a key PG capability the abstraction must emulate differently on MySQL.

entries.title: Drop FOREIGN KEY
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP FOREIGN KEY fk, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t DROP FOREIGN KEY fk_t_p, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;
safeWhen: Always online and metadata-only; works with foreign_key_checks on or off
unsafeWhen: You must reference the CONSTRAINT name (from SHOW CREATE TABLE), not the column or index name
edgeCases: The backing index is NOT dropped automatically; drop it in the same statement if desired: DROP FOREIGN KEY fk, DROP INDEX fk
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG DROP CONSTRAINT for an FK is catalog-only too; behavior matches.

entries.title: Change AUTO_INCREMENT counter
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t AUTO_INCREMENT=10000, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t AUTO_INCREMENT=10000, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;
safeWhen: Raising the counter (metadata-only)
unsafeWhen: Setting it BELOW the current max value is ignored/clamped -- cannot lower into used range
edgeCases: 8.0 persists the auto-increment counter across restarts (fixed the historical reset-on-restart bug)
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG uses sequences (ALTER SEQUENCE ... RESTART / setval), decoupled from the table -- a structurally different identity model.

entries.title: Rename table
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t RENAME TO t2, ALGORITHM=INSTANT  (or RENAME TABLE t TO t2)
entries.safeRewrite: RENAME TABLE t TO t_old, t_new TO t;  -- atomic multi-table swap
safeWhen: Pure metadata operation; RENAME TABLE can swap multiple tables atomically in one statement
unsafeWhen: Privileges/grants are NOT carried to the new name -- reassign manually
Views/stored programs referencing the old name break
edgeCases: The atomic multi-table RENAME is the mechanism gh-ost/pt-osc use for cut-over
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG ALTER TABLE ... RENAME TO is catalog-only but cannot swap two tables atomically in a single statement the way MySQL RENAME TABLE a TO tmp, b TO a can (PG needs a transaction with two renames).

entries.title: Add VIRTUAL generated column
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN v INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (c+1) VIRTUAL, ALGORITHM=INSTANT
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN v INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (c+1) VIRTUAL, ALGORITHM=INSTANT, LOCK=NONE;
safeWhen: VIRTUAL (not stored) columns are computed on read -> metadata-only add/drop
unsafeWhen: Adding a VIRTUAL column to a PARTITIONED table is not in-place
Adding an INDEX on the virtual column at the same time -> that becomes an INPLACE index build (still online) or forces rebuild depending on combination
edgeCases: Dropping a VIRTUAL column is likewise INSTANT/metadata
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG only has STORED generated columns (no VIRTUAL until PG 18); adding one rewrites/scans. MySQL's VIRTUAL add being metadata-only has no direct PG equivalent on older versions -- the abstraction cannot assume symmetry.

entries.title: Add STORED generated column
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN s INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (c+1) STORED, ALGORITHM=COPY
entries.safeRewrite:
-- Small table / window: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN s INT GENERATED ALWAYS AS (c+1) STORED, ALGORITHM=COPY;
-- Large hot table: use gh-ost/pt-osc.
safeWhen: Small table or maintenance window
unsafeWhen: Large table: STORED add is always COPY and write-blocking -> gh-ost/pt-osc
Reordering a STORED column is also COPY
edgeCases: The stored value is materialized for every row at add time, hence the rewrite

entries.title: Toggle column / index visibility (INVISIBLE)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c SET INVISIBLE, ALGORITHM=INSTANT
entries.safeRewrite:
ALTER TABLE t ALTER INDEX ix INVISIBLE, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;  -- test-drop an index safely
ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c SET INVISIBLE, ALGORITHM=INSTANT, LOCK=NONE;
safeWhen: Metadata-only; invisible indexes let you validate a drop before physically removing it
edgeCases: Invisible columns (8.0.23+) are excluded from SELECT * but still writable when named
versionNotes: Index visibility: 8.0.0+. Column visibility: 8.0.23+.
PostgreSQL contrast: PG has no invisible-index or invisible-column feature -- MySQL-only; the abstraction must treat it as unsupported on PG.

entries.title: Convert table CHARACTER SET (e.g. utf8 -> utf8mb4)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t CONVERT TO CHARACTER SET utf8mb4, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=SHARED
entries.lockMode: SHARED when data must be re-encoded (LOCK=NONE not permitted for a real conversion)
entries.safeRewrite:
-- Real re-encoding blocks writes; for a hot table:
gh-ost --alter="CONVERT TO CHARACTER SET utf8mb4" ...  -- or pt-osc
safeWhen: Setting the DEFAULT charset only (CHARACTER SET =, no CONVERT) when column encodings already match -> lighter
Small table or maintenance window for a genuine CONVERT
unsafeWhen: CONVERT TO CHARACTER SET that actually changes byte encoding rebuilds the table and blocks writes -> gh-ost/pt-osc for large tables
Widening utf8mb3->utf8mb4 can push indexed VARCHARs past the 767/3072-byte index-prefix limit -> ALTER fails
edgeCases: utf8mb4 uses 4 bytes/char, so index key length quadruples vs latin1 -- watch max index length
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG has no per-table character set; encoding is fixed per-database at creation. There is no online equivalent -- changing encoding in PG means a dump/reload. A big structural divergence for the abstraction.

entries.title: Change ROW_FORMAT / KEY_BLOCK_SIZE / FORCE rebuild / OPTIMIZE TABLE
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ROW_FORMAT=DYNAMIC, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE  |  ALTER TABLE t FORCE, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t FORCE, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=NONE;  -- online full rebuild; also resets the 64 instant-column row-version count and defragments
safeWhen: Online rebuild dwell + disk headroom acceptable; useful to reclaim space and reset instant row-versions
unsafeWhen: Not in-place for tables with a FULLTEXT index
Large hot table where even LOCK=NONE dwell/commit MDL is risky -> gh-ost/pt-osc
edgeCases: OPTIMIZE TABLE on InnoDB maps to an online ALTER ... FORCE rebuild
ALTER TABLE t ENGINE=InnoDB (null rebuild) is the same online rebuild
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: the analogous compaction is VACUUM FULL / CLUSTER, which take ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (block reads AND writes); pg_repack is the online third-party tool (the gh-ost/pt-osc analog). MySQL's native FORCE is already LOCK=NONE -- an availability advantage.

entries.title: Add / drop / truncate partition (RANGE or LIST)
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD PARTITION (PARTITION p VALUES LESS THAN (...))  |  DROP PARTITION p
entries.lockMode: NONE for RANGE/LIST ADD/DROP/TRUNCATE (reduced locking); SHARED/EXCLUSIVE for HASH/KEY reorg
entries.safeRewrite:
ALTER TABLE t DROP PARTITION p_2023;  -- RANGE/LIST: no copy of surviving data, near-instant
ALTER TABLE t ADD PARTITION (PARTITION p_2027 VALUES LESS THAN (2028));
safeWhen: RANGE/LIST ADD (of an empty new bound) and DROP/TRUNCATE PARTITION: no copy of existing data, reduced locking
Rolling-window retention (drop oldest partition) is the canonical cheap operation
unsafeWhen: REORGANIZE / COALESCE / repartition (PARTITION BY) copies data and does not allow LOCK=NONE
HASH/KEY ADD/COALESCE redistributes rows -> LOCK=SHARED or EXCLUSIVE, blocks writes
DROP PARTITION permanently deletes the rows in it (not just the boundary) -- data loss if misused
edgeCases: OPTIMIZE/REBUILD/REMOVE PARTITIONING rebuild the whole table
gh-ost/pt-osc do not manage partitioning schemes; use native ALTER for these
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG declarative partitioning (10+) uses ATTACH/DETACH PARTITION; DETACH CONCURRENTLY (14+) avoids a long lock. ATTACH validates the partition constraint unless a matching CHECK pre-exists. Different DDL surface entirely from MySQL's ADD/DROP PARTITION.

entries.title: Add an AUTO_INCREMENT column
entries.sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=SHARED
entries.safeRewrite: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN id BIGINT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, ALGORITHM=INPLACE, LOCK=SHARED;  -- rebuilds; writes blocked
safeWhen: Small table or maintenance window; reads continue
unsafeWhen: Large hot table: writes are blocked for the whole rebuild because assigning sequential values requires a consistent snapshot -> gh-ost/pt-osc
edgeCases: Never INSTANT and never LOCK=NONE -- the sequential numbering forces at least SHARED even though other ADD COLUMN forms are INSTANT
Often bundled with adding it as the PK (see add-primary-key), compounding the rebuild
versionNotes: PostgreSQL contrast: PG would ADD COLUMN id bigserial / GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY, which rewrites under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (blocks reads too). MySQL's SHARED keeps reads alive.
